EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow and sleet overspread portions of the I-85 corridor through South Carolina during the afternoon. Precipitation changed quickly to sleet in most areas, before mixing with freezing rain from southwest to northeast during the late afternoon and early evening. Sleet and freezing caused deteriorating road conditions by late evening, when heavy accumulations of sleet and/or freezing rain were reported across much of the area. Most locations saw around a half inch to an inch of sleet, along with around a tenth of an inch of ice accretion. However, areas south of I-85 saw more in the way of freezing rain, with up to a quarter inch of ice accretion reported in addition to light sleet accumulations. Scattered power outages were therefore more concentrated there. Roads became very treacherous and impassable in many areas until melting began on the afternoon of the 17th.

Precipitation that initially began as a mixture of rain and sleet transitioned to freezing rain as temperatures cooled to freezing and below across the southern South Carolina Piedmont. Freezing rain continued through the afternoon and evening, with accumulations primarily confined to elevated surfaces, including trees and power lines. Damaging ice accumulations were reported by late evening. Widespread ice accretion of one quarter to one half inch was reported, resulting in many trees and power lines falling and numerous to widespread power outages.

Light snow associated with a wave of low pressure overspread the foothills and Piedmont of the Carolinas by late evening of the 23rd, and continued through the overnight before tapering off during the morning of the 24th. Accumulations ranged from a dusting to 2 inches, with the highest amounts generally occurring closer to the mountains. Temperatures right around freezing and warm roads resulted in minimal travel issues.

A strong arctic cold front blasted through the southern Appalachians during the afternoon and evening of the 18th, bringing strong winds and bitterly cold air to the region. By mid-evening, sustained winds of 10 to 25 mph combined with air temperatures in the single digits and teens to yield wind chill values in the 0 to -10 range. By daybreak on the 19th, air temperatures in the valleys were near 0 while the high elevations were well below 0. Wind chill values during this time ranged from -5 to -20. The low wind chills continued throughout the 19th, as air temperatures failed to warm above the mid-20s in even the lowest elevations. Wind chills remained no higher than the single digits across most of the area until late morning on the 20th.

A strong arctic cold front blasted through the western Carolinas during the afternoon and evening of the 18th, bringing strong winds and very cold air to the region. Overnight, sustained winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values around 0 by daybreak on the 19th. Although winds diminished, air temperatures failed to warm above the 20s throughout the 19th, while record lows between 0 and 10 above were recorded the morning of the 20th.

After the light snow that fell across portions of the Piedmont and foothills on the morning of the 24th, an area of low pressure moving along the Gulf Coast spread yet another round of snow across the foothills and northern Piedmont of South Carolina, by the evening of the 25th. Snow often mixed with or completely changed to snow at times, undercutting the snowfall rates. Total accumulations ranged from a dusting to an inch south of I-85, to 1 to 3 inches farther north. The snow changed to rain in most areas by midnight.

The snow began to mix with or change to sleet in some areas during the overnight before a transition to rain occurred around daybreak on the 21st. Accumulations ranged from a half inch to an inch, with locally higher amounts of around 2 inches in areas that saw only snow.

After the light snow that fell across portions of the Piedmont and foothills on the morning of the 24th, an area of low pressure moving along the Gulf Coast spread yet another round of snow across the foothills and northern Piedmont of South Carolina, by the evening of the 25th. Snow often mixed with or completely changed to snow at times, undercutting the snowfall rates. Total accumulations ranged from a dusting to an inch south of I-85, to 1 to 3 inches farther north. After the significant snowfall that fell across the mountains on the morning of the 24th, an area of low pressure moving along the Gulf Coast spread yet another round of snow across the southern Appalachians and adjacent foothills during the evening of the 25th. The snow was heavy at times, and quickly accumulated, although occasional mixed rain undercut the totals a bit. Heavy accumulations were reported in many areas by late evening. By the time the snow tapered off during the early morning of the 26th, total accumulations ranged from 2 to 6 inches.

By the time the snow tapered off during the early morning of the 26th, total accumulations ranged from 2 to 6 inches.

Rainfall amounts of 1.5 to 2.5 inches over the eastern Midlands and Upper Pee Dee region produced some urban and small stream flooding.  One person died when he tried to cross a flooding area with his pickup truck.  The truck went into the stream after it went off the shoulder which was washed out.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported one fatality when a pickup truck tried to drive through a flooded area and the went onto the shoulder that was washed out. The truck went into the stream and when the driver tried to get out he was carried away.  The incident occurred around 630am.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through the western Carolinas during the morning and afternoon of the 7th, bringing gusty winds and very cold air to the Piedmont and foothills. By late evening, sustained winds of 5 to 15 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values near 0. Although winds gradually diminished overnight, air temperatures fell to around 10 degrees in many areas by daybreak, and wind chills of 0 to 5 above lingered until temperatures began warming during late morning. However, temperatures remained at or below freezing in many areas throughout the 8th.

An arctic cold front moved through the southern Appalachians during the morning and afternoon of the 7th, bringing strong winds and very cold air to the region. By late evening, sustained winds of 10 to 20 mph combined with air temperatures in the teens to yield wind chill values in the 0 to -5 range in the valleys. By daybreak on the 8th, air temperatures in the valleys were near 0 while the high elevations were well below 0. Wind chill values during this time ranged from 0 to -15 across the valleys, while stronger winds and colder temperatures likely yielded values as low as -25 or colder across the high peaks. The very low wind chills abated throughout the 8th, as temperatures warmed and winds diminished. However, air temperatures remained below freezing throughout the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sleet and snow overspread the mountains and foothills of South Carolina during the afternoon and began to accumulate. Precipitation changed quickly to sleet in most areas, before mixing with freezing rain from southwest to northeast during the late afternoon and early evening. Accumulations of sleet and freezing caused deteriorating road conditions by early evening, when heavy accumulations of sleet and/or freezing rain were reported across much of the area. Most locations saw around a half inch to an inch of sleet, along with around a tenth of an inch of ice accretion. Roads became very treacherous and impassable in many areas until melting began on the afternoon of the 17th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lived and organized line of thunderstorms moved into the area from the west during the late afternoon and evening.  Thunderstorms were able to develop and blossom in the warm and increasingly unstable sector ahead of a cold front. Shear values were also notably high.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey team found evidence of an EF-0 tornado with winds to 75 mph. Two chicken houses were damaged and one other was partially destroyed off of U.S. Highway 401 near the intersection of Lynches River Road. A  trailer near U.S. Highway 401 and West Lynches River Road sustained damage to its underpinning.  Two disease ridden trees alongside the trailer were snapped.  The tornado crossed U.S. Highway 401 knocking down numerous trees. The tornado knocked down more trees which in turn toppled a nearby power pole and power line as it crossed West Lynches Road and lifted. A piece of sheet metal from a chicken house was found 1.75 miles away, in a field off of Hudson Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey team observed minor roof and shingle damage to a house and shed on Newman Swamp Road. Multiple small trees were also blown over at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The environment was characterized as highly unstable. Widespread thunderstorms erupted during the extreme heat of the afternoon and persisted into the evening. Initially severe thunderstorms developed on the Piedmont trough and seabreeze boundary. Later, other severe thunderstorms developed along colliding outflow boundaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Convection organized into a line just upstream and moved from west to east across the forecast area. Portions of the line occasionally bowed out. The thunderstorms moved into an environment where there was a great deal of dry air in the mid-levels. Downdraft CAPE values were high and soundings showed a pronounced inverted-V signature.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reportedly down along Highway 341. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure offshore of the Carolinas deepened and was classified as a Tropical Storm on May 8th. The storm slowly drifted northwest and tropical storm conditions were experienced along the coast from May 9th to the evening of May 10th. Torrential rain caused street flooding with sporadic reports of trees down. The storm made landfall the morning of May 10th along the NC/SC border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Slow moving Tropical Storm Ana produced tropical storm force winds with torrential rain for two days along the coast. A 43 kt gust was recorded at North Myrtle Beach with numerous reports over 35 kts along the coast. Rainfall amounts were between five and six inches.  Some sporadic reports of street flooding were noted.  The tidal surge was recorded at 2.3 feet at the Springmaid Pier with moderate beach erosion observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure aloft with westerly flow, combined with surface high pressure and southwesterly flow, led to a prolonged period of high heat and humidity for the entire southeastern US.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A prolonged period of unseasonably high heat indices blanketed most of the southeastern US. Afternoon temperatures mainly between 95 and 100 combined with dewpoints in the mid 70s produced heat index values in the 105 to 110 range. The heat wave finally broke on Jun 26th with cloud cover and precipitation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms trained across portions of northern Lexington and northwest Richland counties causing rainfalls of 2 to 3 inches in an hour which resulted in flash flooding in the Irmo area. Several streams overflowed and flooded apartments complexes and closed a couple of roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of Bermuda high pressure and an upper ridge created hot temperatures and a very unstable environment. Convection initiated along the Piedmont trough and the seabreeze with additional thunderstorms developing along outflow boundaries. A mid-level shortwave ahead of Tropical Storm Bill moved into the western Carolinas, enhancing the lift across northeast South Carolina and resulting in severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down along U.S. 52.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated to scattered thunderstorms developed across Upstate South Carolina and the eastern Piedmont during the late afternoon and evening. Some of the storms produced severe weather in the form of damaging wind and large hail, especially over eastern York County, which was affected by three different severe storms within about an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: FD reported four trees blown down in the Slater-Marietta area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level shortwave trough passed just north and west of the region throughout the day. Forcing associated with this feature interacted with a deep plume of moisture, helping produce widespread rain along and east of Interstate 95.  Training of showers and thunderstorms led to precipitation rates as high as 3 to 4 inches per hour and resulted in several areas of flash flooding particularly in the Charleston Tri-County region during the morning rush hour.  Many locations reported 4 to 8 inches of rain and an elevated morning high tide exacerbated flooding in coastal locations, including downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Berkeley County 911 Call Center reported flooding on Lighthouse Drive and Pineshadow Drive in Goose Creek. Three cars were also flooded. Flooding was also reported at the intersection of Howe Hall and Keyes Lane. One car was also stalled at the intersection. A ham radio operator also relayed a report of car submerged in water and floating at the intersection of Crystal Springs Drive and Tarpon Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of Bermuda high pressure and an upper ridge created hot temperatures and a very unstable environment. Convection initiated along the Piedmont trough and the seabreeze with additional thunderstorms developing along outflow boundaries. A mid-level shortwave ahead of Tropical Storm Bill moved into the western Carolinas, enhancing the lift across northeast South Carolina and resulting in severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reportedly down on Laurin Willis Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary cold front was positioned to the north with a weak area of low pressure along it. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed in the mid to late afternoon hours within a very unstable airmass. The thunderstorms were aided by the presence of a shortwave trough aloft and 15-20 knots of bulk shear. The thunderstorms produced numerous reports of damaging wind gusts across southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down and in the roadway on Gumwood Drive near Little Capers Road. South Carolina Department of Transportation responded to the downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms developed across central Georgia in the mid afternoon hours and pushed eastward through southeast South Carolina in the evening. The storms developed within a inland trough of low pressure with a upper shortwave trough helping to force the line. A highly unstable airmass with plentiful mid-level dry air created an environment supportive damaging wind gusts across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary cold front was positioned to the north with a weak area of low pressure along it. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed in the mid to late afternoon hours within a very unstable airmass. The thunderstorms were aided by the presence of a shortwave trough aloft and 15-20 knots of bulk shear. The thunderstorms produced numerous reports of damaging wind gusts across southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Beaufort County dispatch reported a tree down near the intersection of Bay Street and Ribaut Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain fell over the Midlands mainly north of I-20. Accumulation amounts were generally an eighth to one quarter of an inch in the central Midlands just north of I-20 and around an half an inch in the northern Midlands.  Trees and powerlines were down in many areas causing short term outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported around a half an inch of ice across the county with trees and powerlines down causing outages.  Even an isolated report near three quarters of an inch in the panhandle. Bridges and overpasses were treacherous.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Cold arctic high pressure centered over the Great Lakes, combined with low pressure tracking off the coast of South Carolina produced snow and ice over the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow began falling before dawn, with trace accumulations noted across the county.  The snow transitioned to freezing rain by afternoon, averaging around a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation, mainly on trees and metal surfaces.  There was an isolated report of two tenths of ice accumulation at the Florence airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low-level jet shifted over the southeastern United States ahead of an approaching cold front. This led to several hours of gusty wind conditions and the issuance of a Wind Advisory over southeast South Carolina from early morning hours through early evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mix of sleet, snow, and freezing rain overspread the South Carolina portion of the Charlotte metro during late afternoon. Sleet became the primary precipitation type during the evening, before precipitation transitioned to freezing rain by late evening. Deteriorating road conditions were reported throughout the evening. By midnight, heavy accumulations of sleet and freezing rain were reported. Most areas saw up to a quarter inch of ice accretion in addition to light sleet accumulations, resulting in scattered power outages. Roads became very treacherous and impassable in many areas until melting began on the afternoon of the 17th.
A small wildfire developed following a prescribed burn in the afternoon hours. Conditions were warm and dry and the wildfire developed overnight. The wildfire spread toward a residential area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 25 acre wildfire occurred on Frost Lane in Huger, within the Francis Marion National Forest. The fire threatened multiple homes in the area, but no property damage occurred. One male victim sustained burns over 70 percent of his body while trying to contain the fire using a tractor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few severe thunderstorms took down some trees and powerlines in the Midlands.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported multiple trees and powerlines down on Shiloh Road near Yarborough Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms rolled through the Midlands and produced damaging winds and some large hail.  Most wind damage was to trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down on Fish Hatchery road just NNE of Pelion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms produced four tornadoes in SC.  One occurred in Edgefield county, one in Aiken County, one in Lexington County, and one in Calhoun County. Therre were two injuries in Calhoun county when a mobile home was destroyed. Numerous trees and homes were damaged in and around the town of Aiken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found an EF1 tornado destroyed a mobile home on Otts Sisters Road injuring two people, damaged another, and also did minor damage to industrial facility. Numerous trees were also down along the path of the tornado.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was down on Bermuda Road near State Road 9 in Fork. Another tree was blown down on Hwy 41. One more tree was down on Leitner Road near old Ebenezer Road.

A tree was blown down on Hwy 34 just before the Pee Dee River Bridge.  Other downed trees were reported on Hwy 177 and Step Road, 524 Danny Thomas Road, New Bridge and Neck Road, and Old Wire Road and Lightwood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous heavy rain showers and thunderstorms moved across the South Carolina Piedmont during the afternoon and evening in association with a warm front. This was after a round of moderate to heavy rain moved across the area early in the day, producing up to 2 inches of rain. A brief severe thunderstorm affected part of Greenwood County during the afternoon, while a quick inch of additional rainfall resulted in flash flooding across portions of York County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended across York County during the evening of the 19th, flooding continued along the Wildcat Creek basin into the early part of the overnight.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Astronomical influences and persistent northeast winds combined to produce a period of higher than normal tides and minor coastal flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The City of Charleston reported several roads and intersections closed due to minor flooding from the evening high tide. These included Hagood Avenue between Fishburne and Horizon Street, America Street between Jackson and Stuart Streets, Morrison Drive between Lee and Stuart Streets, and Drake Street between Chapel and Columbus Streets. The tide at the Charleston Harbor tide gage peaked at 7.34 feet mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ana first formed as a subtropical storm late on 5/7/15.  Ana gradually developed over the course of the next 24 hours and eventually was classified as a tropical storm. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms developing ahead of a cold front moved across northern portions of Upstate South Carolina during the morning, producing isolated wind damage. After a lull of a few hours, more numerous storms developed during the heat of the afternoon, producing more widespread severe weather, mainly in the form of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey found intermittent wind damage, primarily confined to snapped limbs, small-to-medium sized uprooted trees, and a few large trees uprooted, was observed between Lake Hartwell and Highway 187, about 1 mile north of I-85. A couple of homes received minor roof damage in this area due to falling limbs or trees. Sporadic damage to trees continued east/northeast, across Highway 187, to Pine Needle Cir and Hix Dr, where the most significant damage was observed. A small house near the intersection of Hix Rd and Pine Needle Rd had much of the roof lifted and thrown, with debris spread into a field as far as 100 yards. Sporadic damage, mainly in the form of downed limbs, small snapped and uprooted hardwood trees, and larger uprooted softwoods continued northeast across Highway 71. Additional reports of sporadic damage were received from closer to Townville, including the Fair Play Rd area near the Oconee line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous heavy rain showers and thunderstorms moved across the South Carolina Piedmont during the afternoon and evening in association with a warm front. This was after a round of moderate to heavy rain moved across the area early in the day, producing up to 2 inches of rain. A brief severe thunderstorm affected part of Greenwood County during the afternoon, while a quick inch of additional rainfall resulted in flash flooding across portions of York County during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported flash flooding developed across eastern York County after about an inch of rain fell in about an hour associated with an area of thunderstorms. This was on top of 1-2 inches that had already fallen earlier in the day. Automated stream gauges on Wildcat Creek southwest of Rock Hill, and on Big Dutchman Creek north of the city. Multiple roads were closed due to flooding along these creeks and due to poor drainage. Affected roads included Mt Gallant Rd, McConnells Highway at Saluda Rd, and Paraham Rd at Highway 49.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of persistent and strong east/northeast winds, the perigean spring tide and a full moon produced 2 days of elevated high tide cycles along the southeast South Carolina coast.  Major coastal flood stage levels were recorded at the Charleston Harbor (CHTS1) tide gauge on October 27, 2015 and October 28, 2015, which claimed 4th and 8th place respectively on the all-time historic crest list.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The morning high tide in Charleston Harbor caused street flooding in and around the downtown Charleston. Road closures occurred on Wentworth Street, Barre Street, Lockwood Boulevard, Broad Street and Cherry Street. Additional road closures included Morrison Drive from Cooper Street to Stuart Street, Drake Street from South Street to Chapel Street, America Street from Lee Street to Cooper Street and the Lockwood Boulevard exit from Highway 17 North. The intersection of Hagood Avenue and Fishburne Street as well as the outer lanes of Calhoun Street from Lockwood Boulevard to Jonathon Lucas Street were also closed due to tidal flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event affected the Carolinas from October 1-7, 2015. A stalled front offshore combined with deep tropical moisture streaming northwest into the area ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system to the west and Hurricane Joaquin well to the east. This led to historic 4-day rainfall totals with  widespread amounts of 15-20 inches and localized amounts over 25 inches, mainly in the Charleston Tri-County area.  Flash flooding was prevalent for several days and eventually led to Flash Flood Emergencies for Charleston, Berkeley, Dorchester and Colleton counties during the morning hours of October 4, 2015.  Many people had to be rescued by emergency personnel while significant damage occurred to numerous properties and roads. Tides were also historically high due to the recent perigean spring tide and persistent onshore winds, exacerbating and prolonging flooding across the area and along the coast, especially in downtown Charleston. ||The most significant flooding occurred in areas along and near smaller creeks and streams, especially those that were tributaries to larger rivers such as the Edisto, Ashley, Cooper, and Santee. In addition to impacting thousands of homes and businesses, flood waters also damaged many roads and bridges in the area, causing significant travel disruptions that lasted for multiple days. Furthermore, some roads and bridges were so badly damaged that it will likely take weeks to months to repair.||As of 12/31/15, a SCEMD report indicated an estimated total monetary damage of $4,533,337 in Berkeley County, $18,213,860 in Charleston County, $2,030,879 in Colleton County and $6,186,909 in Dorchester County for the flood event. Total costs for each county have been divided equally to each flash flood report within that particular county for this flood event and are considered estimates only.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement and broadcast media reported several roads closed due to flooding on Johns Island and James Island. Roads closed included Brownswood Road between Murraywood Road and Main Road, Bohicket Road at the intersection of Edenvale Road, Towne Street at the intersection of Maybank Highway, Central Park Road and Maybank Highway at the intersection of Hickory Knoll Way. Water was also reported waist deep on the sidewalks along Hickory Knoll Way.

Flash flooding was prevalent for several days and eventually led to Flash Flood Emergencies for Charleston, Berkeley, Dorchester and Colleton counties during the morning hours of October 4, 2015.  Many people had to be rescued by emergency personnel while significant damage occurred to numerous properties and roads. Tides were also historically high due to the recent perigean spring tide and persistent onshore winds, exacerbating and prolonging flooding across the area and along the coast, especially in downtown Charleston. ||The most significant flooding occurred in areas along and near smaller creeks and streams, especially those that were tributaries to larger rivers such as the Edisto, Ashley, Cooper, and Santee. Total costs for each county have been divided equally to each flash flood report within that particular county for this flood event and are considered estimates only.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS employee, an emergency manager, law enforcement and broadcast media reported widespread flooding and roads closed due to flooding in West Ashley. Roads closed included Batten Drive, Highway 17 near the Ponderosa neighborhood, Carolina Bay Drive near the Element at Carolina Bay Apartments and the entrance to Grand Oaks subdivision where 2 cars were also found to be stalled. Ashley Grove Apartments were also reported flooded and many homes were surrounded by water along roads that were impassable. Swift water rescues and evacuations were also reported in the Shadowmoss area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep amplitude east coast upper trough closed off across the southeast states for several days while a surface front meandered across the area. Deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean continued to feed into the area. Tropical moisture from the periphery of Hurricane Joaquin, well off the coast, helped to boost precipitable water values to near record levels. Rainfall amounts averaged 10 to 15 inches across the area during the event. Some locations received record rainfall amounts and there were reports of up to 2 feet of rain. This produced rare flash flooding. Coastal flooding was significant during the time of high tide. Historic and widespread river flooding followed. A few of the rivers remained above flood stage until mid and late October. There were hundreds of lengthy evacuations due to flooding, but no reports of death or injury. There was significant damage to thousands of structures to include homes and businesses and infrastructure such as roads and bridges as well as personal property. There were reports of overtopped levees and earthen dams. Monetary losses were estimated to be several million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flooding was reported along 48th Avenue N. The roadway was subsequently closed. Many of the adjacent neighborhood roads were also flooded. Higher than normal tides were also contributing to the flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local newspaper reported Hudson's Seafood House submerged by flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Midlands...Pee Dee produced flash flooding in across the area. Numerous dams were breached along with numerous bridge and roadways flooded and damaged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: SC Highway Patrol reports Monticello Road at I-20 closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep amplitude east coast upper trough closed off across the southeast states for several days while a surface front meandered across the area. Deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean continued to feed into the area. Tropical moisture from the periphery of Hurricane Joaquin, well off the coast, helped to boost precipitable water values to near record levels. Rainfall amounts averaged 10 to 15 inches across the area during the event. Some locations received record rainfall amounts and there were reports of up to 2 feet of rain. This produced rare flash flooding. Coastal flooding was significant during the time of high tide. Historic and widespread river flooding followed. A few of the rivers remained above flood stage until mid and late October. There were hundreds of lengthy evacuations due to flooding, but no reports of death or injury. There was significant damage to thousands of structures to include homes and businesses and infrastructure such as roads and bridges as well as personal property. There were reports of overtopped levees and earthen dams. Monetary losses were estimated to be several million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood water was reportedly entering homes on South Kaminski Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain for several days produced historic river flooding at several areas in Northeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 15 inches of rain fell over the course of a few days, sending the Lynches River out of its banks and it continued rising to a major flood.  The river crested at 19.73 feet, the highest level since 1945, and the third highest ever.  Over two hundred homes were inundated, with evacuations necessary.  The town of Johnsonville also saw its share of flooding along the Lynches River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, slow-moving thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours across Upstate South Carolina along a backdoor cold front. One of these storms developed over the city of Spartanburg and produced significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms and FD reported significant flash flooding developed on the north side of the city of Spartanburg after 3 to 5 inches of rain fell in only a couple of hours. Multiple roads were closed due to poor drainage flooding and due to Chinquapin Creek and other small tributaries overflowing their banks. A 56-year-old woman drowned in her vehicle when she drove into water on New Cut Rd. A man was injured when he drove his truck into a collapsed culvert on Hayne St near Ethel Rd. Water from Chinquapin Creek entered homes on Pearson St. Water from an overflowing tributary of Lawsons Fork Creek entered about a dozen apartment units at USC Upstate. Closed roads included Asheville Highway, Howard St, Valley Falls Rd, New Cut Rd and I-85 Business. Wet soil and erosion caused a tree to fell on a home in the Una Community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, slow-moving thunderstorms developed during the early morning hours across Upstate South Carolina along a backdoor cold front. One of these storms developed over the city of Spartanburg and produced significant flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Although heavy rain ended across the Spartanburg area by the pre-dawn hours, runoff Lawsons Fork Creek remaining above flood stage for a few more hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More significant rain fell across the area. This rainfall was on the heels of historic rainfall the weekend prior. As a result, rainfall atop saturated ground led to more flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Thurgood Marshall Hwy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered, slow-moving thunderstorms developed across the South Carolina foothills in the late afternoon and evening in the vicinity of a nearly stationary front. One slow moving storm caused localized flash flooding across southwest Pickens County, after 2 to 4 inches of rain fell in a short period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding developed in the Clemson and Central areas after a slow-moving thunderstorm produced 2 to 4 inches of rain in a short period of time. Small tributaries of Twelve Mile Creek apparently overflowed and flooded roads in the Woodland Heights and Dellwood neighborhoods of Clemson. In Central, Maw Brdige Rd, Blue Jay Rd, Spring Forest Dr and Brock St were all closed due to poor drainage or small stream flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: More significant rain fell across the area. This rainfall was on the heels of historic rainfall the weekend prior. As a result, rainfall atop saturated ground led to more flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on Hwy 17.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several road were flooded in the city of Georgetown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving area of upper level low pressure tapped into a tropical moisture feed associated with Hurricane Joaquin (located across the western Atlantic, well off the southeast coast) to produce periods of moderate to heavy rainfall across Upstate South Carolina throughout the 3rd and 4th. The main area impacted was the lower Piedmont, which saw storm total amounts in the 7-10 inches range. Much of that fell on the night of the 3rd and early on the 4th, when the most significant flooding developed over these areas. In addition to the flooding, the combination of saturated soils and winds gusting in excess of 20 mph caused many trees to fall across the area, with some trees falling on homes and vehicles. The Upstate was spared the worst of the heavy rain and flooding, as portions of the South Carolina midlands and coastal plain saw storm total amounts of 15-25 inches during this historic event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After moderate to occasionally heavy rain fell across the area throughout the 3rd, an area of very heavy rain moved out of the midlands during the evening of the 3rd, increasing rainfall rates enough to cause flash flooding across portions of Greenwood County. Wilson Creek overflowed its banks and flooded Siloam Church Rd, while Cobb Rd was closed due to flooding along a tributary to Rocky Creek.

County comms reported flooding along McKenley Creek and its tributaries, with water covering two bridges on McKenley Creek Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving area of upper level low pressure tapped into a tropical moisture feed associated with Hurricane Joaquin (located across the western Atlantic, well off the southeast coast) to produce periods of moderate to heavy rainfall across Upstate South Carolina throughout the 3rd and 4th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: After early morning flash flooding, rain rates moderated across the lower Piedmont, but flooding continued and gradually expanded, with high water conditions remaining in place throughout much of the 4th. At the height of the event, at least a dozen roads were closed due to urban/drainage flooding issues or due to lingering high flow conditions on small streams, especially in the city of Greenwood and surrounding areas. In addition to the flooding, the combination of saturated soils and winds gusting in excess of 20 mph caused numerous trees to fall throughout this period of time. Several trees fell on homes in the city of Greenwood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep amplitude east coast upper trough closed off across the southeast states for several days while a surface front meandered across the area. Deep moisture from the Gulf of Mexico and Atlantic Ocean continued to feed into the area. Tropical moisture from the periphery of Hurricane Joaquin, well off the coast, helped to boost precipitable water values to near record levels. Rainfall amounts averaged 10 to 15 inches across the area during the event. Some locations received record rainfall amounts and there were reports of up to 2 feet of rain. This produced rare flash flooding. Coastal flooding was significant during the time of high tide. Historic and widespread river flooding followed. A few of the rivers remained above flood stage until mid and late October. There were hundreds of lengthy evacuations due to flooding, but no reports of death or injury. There was significant damage to thousands of structures to include homes and businesses and infrastructure such as roads and bridges as well as personal property. There were reports of overtopped levees and earthen dams. Monetary losses were estimated to be several million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along portions of Rogers Road and Sunset Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain for several days produced historic river flooding at several areas in Northeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 15 inches of rain fell over a few days on the basins to the Waccamaw River at Conway.  The river subsequently rose to a major flood, cresting at 16.20 feet, the third highest crest of all time, and the highest crest since Hurricane Floyd.  Over 400 homes were inundated, with evacuations necessary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rain for several days produced historic river flooding at several areas in Northeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 10 to 15 inches of rain fell over a three day period, causing the Black River at Kingstree to rise to a major flood.  The river crested at 22.65 feet, beating the previous record crest in 1973 by nearly three feet.  Several hundred homes were evacuated due to inundation.  The town of Quinby had moderate flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep amplitude east coast upper trough closed off across the southeast states for several days while a surface front meandered across the area. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A historic flooding event affected the Carolinas from October 1-7, 2015. A stalled front offshore combined with deep tropical moisture streaming northwest into the area ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system to the west and Hurricane Joaquin well to the east. This led to historic 4-day rainfall totals with  widespread amounts of 15-20 inches and localized amounts over 25 inches, mainly in the Charleston Tri-County area.  Flash flooding was prevalent for several days and eventually led to Flash Flood Emergencies for Charleston, Berkeley, Dorchester and Colleton counties during the morning hours of October 4, 2015.  Many people had to be rescued by emergency personnel while significant damage occurred to numerous properties and roads. Tides were also historically high due to the recent perigean spring tide and persistent onshore winds, exacerbating and prolonging flooding across the area and along the coast, especially in downtown Charleston. ||The most significant flooding occurred in areas along and near smaller creeks and streams, especially those that were tributaries to larger rivers such as the Edisto, Ashley, Cooper, and Santee. In addition to impacting thousands of homes and businesses, flood waters also damaged many roads and bridges in the area, causing significant travel disruptions that lasted for multiple days. Furthermore, some roads and bridges were so badly damaged that it will likely take weeks to months to repair.||As of 12/31/15, a SCEMD report indicated an estimated total monetary damage of $4,533,337 in Berkeley County, $18,213,860 in Charleston County, $2,030,879 in Colleton County and $6,186,909 in Dorchester County for the flood event. Total costs for each county have been divided equally to each flash flood report within that particular county for this flood event and are considered estimates only.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The morning high tide at the Charleston Harbor tide gauge peaked at 7.61 feet mean lower low water and caused street flooding in the downtown area. This included road closures at the intersections of Wentworth Street and Barre Street, South Market Street and Church Street, Lockwood Boulevard and Broad Street and on East Bay Street between Jackson and Cooper.

Water backed up a drainage canal, resulting in waist deep water in the middle of Arbor Oaks Drive. Approximately 2 feet of water was in 40 to 50 homes along Arbor Oaks Drive. Around 100 people were rescued from flooded areas along Old Trolley Road, Ladson Road and Miles Jamison Road.

Tides were also historically high due to the recent perigean spring tide and persistent onshore winds, exacerbating and prolonging flooding across the area and along the coast, especially in downtown Charleston. ||The most significant flooding occurred in areas along and near smaller creeks and streams, especially those that were tributaries to larger rivers such as the Edisto, Ashley, Cooper, and Santee. Furthermore, some roads and bridges were so badly damaged that it will likely take weeks to months to repair.||As of 12/31/15, a SCEMD report indicated an estimated total monetary damage of $4,533,337 in Berkeley County, $18,213,860 in Charleston County, $2,030,879 in Colleton County and $6,186,909 in Dorchester County for the flood event. Total costs for each county have been divided equally to each flash flood report within that particular county for this flood event and are considered estimates only.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported the Kiawah Island Main Gate closed to entry traffic due to flooding.

A stalled front offshore combined with deep tropical moisture streaming northwest into the area ahead of a strong upper level low pressure system to the west and Hurricane Joaquin well to the east. This led to historic 4-day rainfall totals with widespread amounts of 15-20 inches and localized amounts over 25 inches, mainly in the Charleston Tri-County area. Flash flooding was prevalent for several days and eventually led to Flash Flood Emergencies for Charleston, Berkeley, Dorchester and Colleton counties during the morning hours of October 4, 2015. Many people had to be rescued by emergency personnel while significant damage occurred to numerous properties and roads. Tides were also historically high due to the recent perigean spring tide and persistent onshore winds, exacerbating and prolonging flooding across the area and along the coast, especially in downtown Charleston.||The most significant flooding occurred in areas along and near smaller creeks and streams, especially those that were tributaries to larger rivers such as the Edisto, Ashley, Cooper, and Santee. In addition to impacting thousands of homes and businesses, flood waters also damaged many roads and bridges in the area, causing significant travel disruptions that lasted for multiple days. Furthermore, some roads and bridges were so badly damaged that it will likely take weeks to months to repair.||As of 12/31/15, a SCEMD report indicated an estimated total monetary damage of $4,533,337 in Berkeley County, $18,213,860 in Charleston County, $2,030,879 in Colleton County and $6,186,909 in Dorchester County from the flood event.  Total costs for each county have been divided equally to each flash flood report within that particular county for this flood event and are considered estimates only.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The afternoon high tide at the Charleston Harbor tide gauge peaked at 7.52 feet mean lower low water. Law enforcement reported South Market Street being closed due to high water between Church Street and State Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was highly unstable with high mixed layer CAPE values and steep lapse rates. The environment was also characterized by high precipitable water values. Developing thunderstorms were able to utilize these ingredients, resulting in pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were reported down along Pamplico Highway causing traffic disruptions. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was highly unstable with high mixed layer CAPE values and steep lapse rates. The environment was also characterized by high precipitable water values. Developing thunderstorms were able to utilize these ingredients, resulting in pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS at the Florence Regional Airport measured a 64 mph wind gust. Some of the windows at the airport were reportedly blown out. The terminal was evacuated. Trees were reported down on airport property.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was highly unstable with high mixed layer CAPE values and steep lapse rates. The environment was also characterized by high precipitable water values. Developing thunderstorms were able to utilize these ingredients, resulting in pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of thunderstorms moved out of North Carolina during the late afternoon and produced a brief severe weather across portions of Spartanburg and Cherokee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail near Landrum.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of thunderstorms moved out of North Carolina during the late afternoon and produced a brief severe weather across portions of Spartanburg and Cherokee Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper reported multiple trees blown down in Gaffney, including along Thompson St and E Jefferies St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Upstate South Carolina during the afternoon. While most of the severe weather occurred in North Carolina, brief severe storms  affected portions of the Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported multiple large tree limbs blown down on Emerson St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was highly unstable with high mixed layer CAPE values and steep lapse rates. The environment was also characterized by high precipitable water values. Developing thunderstorms were able to utilize these ingredients, resulting in pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were either reported down or snapped along E Palmetto Street. Some trees fell onto homes and cars causing some damage. There were numerous people without power. There was reportedly minor damage to small sheds as well. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Upstate South Carolina during the afternoon. While most of the severe weather occurred in North Carolina, brief severe storms  affected portions of the Piedmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Newspaper reported multiple trees blown down along Shelby Highway and one metal sign blown over on I-85.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the evening across the extreme northern South Carolina Piedmont. One storm produced brief damaging winds across Cherokee County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported at least one tree blown down at the I-85 South Carolina Welcome Center. FD reported a tree blown down on Antioch Rd and another tree down on Elm Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front and sea breeze boundary. The environment was very unstable. A shortwave lifting northeast from the Gulf Coast and increasing diffluence aloft helped to produce a couple severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on Johnson Shelly Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front and sea breeze boundary. The environment was very unstable. A shortwave lifting northeast from the Gulf Coast and increasing diffluence aloft helped to produce a couple severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reportedly down. Two barns were also reported destroyed. The damage occurred on Highway 134 near Pee Dee Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed along a stationary front and sea breeze boundary. The environment was very unstable. A shortwave lifting northeast from the Gulf Coast and increasing diffluence aloft helped to produce a couple severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of quarters was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms produced four tornadoes in SC.  One occurred in Edgefield county, one in Aiken County, one in Lexington County, and one in Calhoun County. Therre were two injuries in Calhoun county when a mobile home was destroyed. Numerous trees and homes were damaged in and around the town of Aiken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found an EF1 tornado took down numerous trees and damaged several homes on the south side of Lake Murray.  The tornado continued across the lake and touched down on Shadowood Road taking down a few trees, some on homes, and also lifted a pontoon boat out of the water dropping it down on a dock on its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms developed across central Georgia in the mid afternoon hours and pushed eastward through southeast South Carolina in the evening. The storms developed within a inland trough of low pressure with a upper shortwave trough helping to force the line. A highly unstable airmass with plentiful mid-level dry air created an environment supportive damaging wind gusts across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Highway Patrol reported a tree down on Interstate 95 northbound at mile marker 46.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered thunderstorms developed across the mountains and foothills during the late afternoon and early evening. A couple of storms produced localized large hail and damaging winds across Oconee and Anderson Counties. Some of the storms moved into the lower Piedmont late in the evening, producing urban and small stream flash flooding in the Greenwood area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported multiple trees blown down along Tokeena Rd between Seneca and Fair Play.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved into the area during the evening along and ahead of a pre-frontal trough. The clusters of thunderstorms were moving into an environment characterized by significant shear in the low and mid levels.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reportedly in the roadway at the intersection of Georgetown Road and Wachovia Hills Road. The time was estimated based on radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms moved into Upstate South Carolina from northeast Georgia during the afternoon and produced localized severe weather in the form of damaging winds and large hail, mainly over Anderson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported penny size hail near Sandy Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced wind damage that took down trees and powerlines causing some power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported trees and powerlines down with outages north and northeast of Saluda to the Newberry county line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved through the Midlands of SC and produced some large hail and wind damage...mainly trees taken down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported hail as large as golf ball size in Blythewood along with high winds and torrential rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Supercell thunderstorms produced four tornadoes in SC.  One occurred in Edgefield county, one in Aiken County, one in Lexington County, and one in Calhoun County. Therre were two injuries in Calhoun county when a mobile home was destroyed. Numerous trees and homes were damaged in and around the town of Aiken.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS storm survey found an EF2 tornado touched down near Glenwood Drive and continued across south and southeast Aiken to I-20. Numerous trees and many homes, vehicles, and other structures were damaged on the south side of Aiken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across the mountains and foothills during the early afternoon and moved southeast into the Piedmont during mid-to-late afternoon. A few of the storms produced brief severe weather in the form of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Spotter reported a large tree blown down on the campus of Presbyterian College in Clinton. County comms reported another tree blown down on I-26 near Clinton, and another tree down near the intersection of Highways 56 and 66 near Joanna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced wind damage that took down trees and powerlines causing some power outages.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees down on Hwy 378 about 4 miles east of McCormick.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of strong to severe thunderstorms pushes into Upstate South Carolina from north Georgia during the evening. The line of storms gradually weakened as it moved east, but not before producing localized wind damage across western portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol, media, and public reported multiple trees and some power lines blown down across western Greenville County, from the Golden Grove area, through West Greenville, to Berea, to Travelers Rest. A tree fell on a home in the Berea area and some siding was stripped from a home in the Judson community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the evening across the eastern half of Upstate South Carolina. Some of the storms produced brief severe weather, mainly in the form of marginally large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported quarter size hail in the Roebuck area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced wind damage around the Midlands and Pee Dee regions taking down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway Patrol reported trees down on Hwy 5 at Bon Rea Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms developed across central Georgia in the mid afternoon hours and pushed eastward through southeast South Carolina in the evening. The storms developed within a inland trough of low pressure with a upper shortwave trough helping to force the line. A highly unstable airmass with plentiful mid-level dry air created an environment supportive damaging wind gusts across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down in the Burton area and one of the trees fell on a house. Damage to the home was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed in the early to mid afternoon hours and produced damaging wind gusts as they moved toward the coast. The thunderstorms developed within an inland surface trough, in a very unstable environment due to dew points near 80 and temperatures in the upper 90's. Outflow boundary interactions also helped provide enhancements to thunderstorm strength.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A coop observer on Edisto Island reported a large tree blown down along Chisolm Plantation Road. Also, a pine tree, approximately 10 inches in diamter, was snapped off halfway up off Lottie Pope Road on Edisto Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed starting in the early afternoon hours and produced damaging wind gusts across southeast South Carolina before pushing off the coast. The storms developed within northwest flow aloft with local enhancements provided by mesoscale boundary interactions. Several of the storms became severe and  produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large oak tree split in half along South Aviation Avenue between Aviation Avenue and Remount Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed after midnight following an afternoon where the atmosphere was sufficiently capped. Thunderstorms developed along mesoscale boundaries and produced damaging wind gusts and even some hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Colleton County Emergency Management reported a tree blown down across both lanes at the intersection of Poplar and Hayden Streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The atmosphere was highly unstable with high mixed layer CAPE values and steep lapse rates. The environment was also characterized by high precipitable water values. Developing thunderstorms were able to utilize these ingredients, resulting in pulse severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail to the size of nickels was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong line of thunderstorms rapidly developed in the early afternoon hours within a inland surface trough. The line of thunderstorms quickly moved to the southeast and produced numerous damaging wind gusts across much of southeast South Carolina all the way to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South Carolina Highway Patrol reported that a tree was blown down onto Murdaugh Street at the Walters Place intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line of thunderstorms moved south through the Midlands and produced wind damage taking down trees and powerlines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatch reported trees and powerlines down on Calvary Church Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved though the Midlands and produced some large hail, wind damage, and very intense rains that produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: USGS cameras showed the intersection of Main and Whaley flooded and impassable. The Rocky Branch Creek peaked at 9.81 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved though the Midlands and produced some large hail, wind damage, and very intense rains that produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported 3 to 4 feet of water covering the 5300 block of Broad River Road near  Youth Services Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved though the Midlands and produced some large hail, wind damage, and very intense rains that produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported significant road flooding at Broad River Road and Royal Tower Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved though the Midlands and produced some large hail, wind damage, and very intense rains that produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social Media report via Facebook showed a home with a flooded basement.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of periods of persistent and strong northeast winds, the proxigean spring tides, and a full moon produced several days of elevated high tides. A few of the high tide cycles produced flooding along the southeast South Carolina coast, especially downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The evening high tide at the Charleston Harbor tide gauge peaked at 7.44 feet mean lower low water and caused some street flooding in the downtown area. This included Morrison Drive being closed between Jackson Street and Lee Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of periods of persistent and strong northeast winds, the proxigean spring tides, and a full moon produced several days of elevated high tides. A few of the high tide cycles produced flooding along the southeast South Carolina coast, especially downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The evening high tide at the Charleston Harbor tide gauge peaked at 7.73 feet mean lower low water and caused some street flooding in the downtown area. Impacted roadways included Lockwood Boulevard, Broad Street, Barre Street, Morrison Drive, South Market Street, Calhoun Street, Long Point Road, Hagood Avenue, and Fishburne Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved though the Midlands and produced some large hail, wind damage, and very intense rains that produced flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported flooding at the Country Walk Apartments at 408 Fox Fire Drive near Bower Parkway and Piney Grove Road. Road was impassable with one stranded vehicle.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported nickel to quarter size hail with a few pieces up to half dollar size near Jalapa off of US 76.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large mid and upper level low helped to draw deep moisture into southeast South Carolina while a low pressure system at the surface became centered off the southeast coast. The low tracked slowly west-northwest and aided in backing the low level wind field along the South Carolina coast. Overnight, coastal front lifted northward and allowed for surface based instability to spread inland from the adjacent coastal waters. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed over the coastal waters late in the evening and a few of these exhibited supercell characteristics including strong low level rotation. One thunderstorm in particular quickly strengthened as it moved onshore at Kiawah Island near the coastal front. The storm deepened and rapidly developed strengthening low and mid-level rotation. The thunderstorm soon showed numerous radar characteristics suggestive of a ongoing tornado. Ultimately the thunderstorm spawned a tornado that created a 7 mile path of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team confirmed a tornado on Johns Island. The tornado touched down near Cane Slash Road where one home had a portion of its roof ripped off. The tornado then tracked to the north-northwest and crossed Maybank Highway and Brownswood Road, causing minor damage to a few homes and extensive damage to trees. The tornado then tracked further north-northwest and caused the most significant damage along its path on Sonny Boy Lane, where damage to two homes was consistent with a high end EF-2 tornado. At one home, only a small portion of the roof was left intact as the rest of the roof and most of the exterior walls of the well built brick home were knocked down. A large portion of this roof and a ground level air condition unit were hurled about 150 yards. At another well built home across the street, the entire back portion of the two story house was ripped off.  From there, the tornado continued its north-northwest track crossing River Road, the Stono River, Main Road, and eventually Highway 17 where it dissipated. Along this portion of the path the tornado produced widespread destruction to numerous acres of trees and damage to the roofs of two businesses near Highway 17. According to a damage assessment performed by the Charleston County Building Services Department, 51 total structures were damaged including 33 with moderate or worse damage and 18 with only minor damage.  The total damage estimate resulting solely from structures was $1,539,000. Thousands and thousands of trees were uprooted or snapped off with many falling onto structures, vehicles, and roadways. The tree damage was so extensive and widespread that several roads along the path were impassable for a few days following the event.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A combination of periods of persistent and strong northeast winds, the upcoming proxigean spring tides, and the upcoming full moon produced an elevated high tide cycle. This resulted in flooding along the southeast South Carolina coast, especially downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Charleston Police Department reported street flooding and some road closures due to the evening high tide. This included stalled vehicles along East Bay Street and Morrison Drive. Closed roads included America Street between Lee and Cooper, Hagood Avenue between Line and Allway Streets, and the intersection of Barre and Wentworth Streets. The high tide at the Charleston Harbor Tide gauge reached 7.56 feet mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level shortwave trough passed just north and west of the region throughout the day. Forcing associated with this feature interacted with a deep plume of moisture, helping produce widespread rain along and east of Interstate 95.  Training of showers and thunderstorms led to precipitation rates as high as 3 to 4 inches per hour and resulted in several areas of flash flooding particularly in the Charleston Tri-County region during the morning rush hour.  Many locations reported 4 to 8 inches of rain and an elevated morning high tide exacerbated flooding in coastal locations, including downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement reported the intersection of Lowcountry Drive and Chippa Willow Road closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level shortwave trough passed just north and west of the region throughout the day. Forcing associated with this feature interacted with a deep plume of moisture, helping produce widespread rain along and east of Interstate 95.  Training of showers and thunderstorms led to precipitation rates as high as 3 to 4 inches per hour and resulted in several areas of flash flooding particularly in the Charleston Tri-County region during the morning rush hour.  Many locations reported 4 to 8 inches of rain and an elevated morning high tide exacerbated flooding in coastal locations, including downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media, social media as well as the Charleston County 911 Call Center and public reported numerous locations of flooding due to heavy rainfall. Highway 61 was flooded and closed at the intersection of Muirfield Parkway and Greenridge Road. Part of an embankment collapsed near the intersection of Interstate 526 and Dorchester Road. Water was reported entering homes along Shadowmoss Parkway and the Charleston County Swiftwater Rescue Team was deployed to Shadowmoss to evacuate a residence. Severe flooding was reported within the Charlestowne Village Mobile Home Park.  Approximately 1 to 2 feet of water was in the first floor of Dorchester Gardens Apartments and 2 to 3 feet of water was in the first floor of The Reserve At Ashley River Apartments off Dorchester Road. Approximately 2 feet of water was also reported in a furnishings business off Dorchester Road with significant monetary damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture levels along with the continuation of moisture convergence, helped support training of thunderstorms over downtown Charleston for a few hours during mid to late afternoon.  Despite an approaching low tide, extensive flash flooding occurred as 2.50 to 3.75 inches of rain fell within a couple hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Charleston Police Department and the City of Charleston Emergency Manager reported numerous roads flooded and closed in downtown Charleston. Streets closed included parts of Fishburne, King, Carolina, Line, Sumter, Beaufain, Barre, Calhoun, Ogier, Pitt, Moultrie, President, America, South, Bogard, Huger, Smith, Morris, Ashley Avenue and the Crosstown. Many cars were flooded or stalled. Many homes and businesses were surrounded by water. The Market was also reported flooded and under water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level shortwave trough passed just north and west of the region throughout the day. Forcing associated with this feature interacted with a deep plume of moisture, helping produce widespread rain along and east of Interstate 95.  Training of showers and thunderstorms led to precipitation rates as high as 3 to 4 inches per hour and resulted in several areas of flash flooding particularly in the Charleston Tri-County region during the morning rush hour.  Many locations reported 4 to 8 inches of rain and an elevated morning high tide exacerbated flooding in coastal locations, including downtown Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported the intersection of Greenridge Road and Crossroads Drive closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain redeveloped across northern South Carolina on the 23rd, resulting in the second round of flooding across York County in less than 36 hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After the flooding that developed on the 22nd, another round of heavy rain moved into York County on the 23rd, resulting in flooding of the same streams and locations that were affected on the 22nd. Stream gauges on Big Dutchman Creek and Wildcat Creek overflowed established flood stages near Rock Hill. Big Dutchman likely flooded part of W Oak Dr, as well as the back yards of some homes on Mt Gallant Rd. Wildcat Creek flooded part of McConnells Highway and Saluda Rd near Highway 324.

Affected streets included Cherry Street from Bee Street to Cannon Street, Ashley Avenue from Bennett Street to Calhoun Street, Hagood Street at Horizon Avenue, Wentworth Street at Berry Street, Morrison Street from Harris Street to Highway 17, a portion of Morrison Street at Huger Street, Washington Street from Hasell Street to Lauren's Street, South Market Street from Church Street to East Bay Street, Hagood Street from Fishburne Street to Line Street, Ashley Avenue from Tradd Street to Briad Street, and Central Park Road from Riverland Road to Fleming Road. The morning high tide in Charleston Harbor peaked at 7.33 feet Mean Lower Low Water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing along a front across the Southeast coast spread moisture and rain into northern South Carolina throughout the 9th. With stream levels already running high due to recent wet weather, minor flooding developed across portions of York County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County comms reported flooding developed across portions of southern York County after about 2 inches of rain fell in less than 24 hours. Closed roads included Wilson Chapel in Sharon, Gunsmoke Rd in McConnells (possibly due to an overflowing tributary of Conrad Creek, and Stallcup Rd (possibly due to overflowing Stony Fork). Additionally, a stream gauge on Wildcat Creek exceeded established flood stage, indicating flooding of McConnells Highway and of the Highway 324/72 intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of shortwaves rounding the southern periphery of a longwave trough along with upper jet divergence helped spawn thunderstorms within an unseasonably warm and moist airmass over southeast South Carolina.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service office in Charleston, South Carolina examined photographs provided by Berkeley County Emergency Management and drone footage provided by Skyview Aerial Solutions LLC to determine an EF0 tornado with estimated maximum wind speeds around 70 mph occurred for one minute at Carolina Nurseries, about 3 miles south southwest of Moncks Corner, South Carolina. The tornado initially formed at or near Emerald Isle Drive. Straight-line wind damage just south of this road included numerous wooden pallets that were tossed toward the northeast. A telephone pole was blown over and a two by six piece of wood pierced the east side of a building just north of Emerald Isle Drive. Roofing material was also missing from a building situated just north of Emerald Isle Drive. Emergency management personnel reported a 200 square foot shed destroyed with its roof found 300 feet from the original location. Multiple carts weighing 150 pounds each were also tossed away from the shed sight.  Vegetation revealed a swirling, convergent damage signature produced by a weak tornadic circulation farther north-northeast along the tornado path.
